card.indications: Used with other antitubercular drugs to treat active tuberculosis caused by susceptible Mycobacterium tuberculosis. It should not be used alone because resistance develops rapidly.
card.mechanism: Inhibits mycobacterial arabinosyl transferases, disrupting synthesis of the arabinogalactan component of the cell wall. It is primarily bacteriostatic against actively growing mycobacteria.
card.dosing: Dosing is weight based and depends on whether therapy is daily or intermittent, prior treatment, age, renal function, and the full tuberculosis regimen. Adults commonly receive about 15 mg/kg once daily in initial therapy; higher intermittent or retreatment doses require specialist oversight. Renal impairment usually requires interval adjustment.
card.routeAdministration: Give orally at the same time each day as part of the complete multidrug regimen. May be taken with food if stomach upset occurs. Verify baseline visual acuity and color discrimination before therapy and reinforce adherence to all companion drugs.
card.contraindications: Known optic neuritis unless clinical judgment determines that use is essential; inability to recognize or report visual changes when monitoring cannot be performed; and hypersensitivity to ethambutol.
card.warnings: Can cause dose- and duration-related optic neuritis with decreased visual acuity, central or peripheral scotoma, and red-green color discrimination changes. Risk increases with renal impairment because the drug accumulates. Hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy, and serious hypersensitivity are less common.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, headache, dizziness, malaise, joint pain, and elevated uric acid.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Optic neuritis with potentially irreversible vision loss, severe hypersensitivity, hepatotoxicity, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia, peripheral neuropathy, and gout related to hyperuricemia.
card.interactions: Aluminum-containing antacids may reduce absorption and should be separated from the dose. Other potentially neurotoxic or hepatotoxic antitubercular drugs can add to toxicity.
card.monitoring: Obtain baseline and periodic visual acuity and red-green color testing, especially with prolonged therapy or doses above standard daily therapy. Monitor renal function, liver tests, uric acid when indicated, adherence, sputum or culture response, and symptoms of neuropathy.
card.patientTeaching: Take every dose with the other tuberculosis medicines and never stop independently. Report blurred vision, decreased vision, eye pain, blind spots, or difficulty distinguishing red from green immediately. Do not rely on routine eyeglasses changes to correct new symptoms.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if nausea occurs. Separate aluminum-containing antacids by several hours. Maintain hydration, and discuss purine intake if gout or marked hyperuricemia develops.

card.indications: Treatment of active tuberculosis as part of a multidrug regimen and treatment of latent tuberculosis infection according to the selected public-health regimen.
card.mechanism: After activation within susceptible mycobacteria, inhibits synthesis of mycolic acids needed for the mycobacterial cell wall. It is bactericidal against actively dividing organisms and bacteriostatic against resting organisms.
card.dosing: Adult daily dosing for active or latent infection is commonly 5 mg/kg up to 300 mg daily, while intermittent regimens use higher weight-based doses under directly observed or protocol-based therapy. Pediatric dosing is weight based. Adjustments and regimen duration depend on indication, age, liver risk, and companion drugs.
card.routeAdministration: Give on an empty stomach, usually 1 hour before or 2 hours after food, because food reduces absorption. If severe GI intolerance prevents fasting administration, follow prescriber direction. Pyridoxine is often prescribed for patients at risk for neuropathy.
card.contraindications: Acute liver disease, previous severe isoniazid-associated hepatic injury, serious hypersensitivity, or prior severe drug reaction such as hepatitis, fever, chills, or arthritis attributed to isoniazid.
card.warnings: Boxed warning: severe and sometimes fatal hepatitis can occur even after months of therapy. Risk increases with age, alcohol use, chronic liver disease, postpartum status, and use of other hepatotoxic drugs. Peripheral neuropathy, seizures, psychosis, severe skin reactions, and blood dyscrasias may occur.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, epigastric discomfort, fatigue, headache, dizziness, mild rash, and transient liver-enzyme elevation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Fulminant hepatitis, liver failure, severe peripheral neuropathy, seizures, psychosis, lupus-like syndrome, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia, severe hypersensitivity, and metabolic acidosis in overdose.
card.interactions: Alcohol and other hepatotoxic drugs increase liver risk. Isoniazid inhibits metabolism of phenytoin, carbamazepine, and some benzodiazepines. Aluminum antacids reduce absorption. Tyramine- or histamine-rich foods may rarely provoke flushing, headache, palpitations, or hypotension.
card.monitoring: Assess baseline liver-risk factors and obtain liver tests when indicated; repeat promptly for symptoms or in high-risk patients. Monitor for jaundice, fatigue, anorexia, nausea, dark urine, neuropathy, adherence, and clinical or microbiologic response.
card.patientTeaching: Stop the medication and contact the clinician immediately for loss of appetite, persistent nausea, vomiting, unusual fatigue, dark urine, pale stools, right-upper-quadrant pain, or jaundice. Avoid alcohol. Take pyridoxine exactly as ordered and report numbness or tingling.
card.nutrition: Prefer an empty stomach. Separate aluminum antacids by at least 1 hour. Avoid alcohol. Limit large amounts of tyramine- or histamine-rich foods if flushing, headache, palpitations, or other reactions occur.

card.indications: Reduction of elevated intraocular pressure in patients with open-angle glaucoma or ocular hypertension.
card.mechanism: Blocks beta1- and beta2-adrenergic receptors in the ciliary body, reducing aqueous-humor production and lowering intraocular pressure. Systemic absorption can still produce cardiac and respiratory beta-blockade.
card.dosing: Product-specific dosing varies by concentration and formulation. Conventional solution is commonly one drop in the affected eye once or twice daily; gel-forming products are commonly once daily. Use the lowest effective frequency based on intraocular-pressure response.
card.routeAdministration: Wash hands, avoid touching the dropper tip, instill the prescribed drop, close the eye gently, and apply pressure to the nasolacrimal duct for 1 to 2 minutes to reduce systemic absorption. Separate other ophthalmic medications by at least 5 to 10 minutes. Remove contact lenses when product labeling requires it.
card.contraindications: Bronchial asthma or history of asthma, severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease with bronchospasm, sinus bradycardia, second- or third-degree AV block without a pacemaker, overt cardiac failure, cardiogenic shock, or hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Although used in the eye, timolol is systemically absorbed and can cause fatal bronchospasm, bradycardia, heart block, worsening heart failure, and masking of hypoglycemia or hyperthyroidism. Abrupt withdrawal before surgery or in patients with cardiovascular disease requires clinician guidance.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Transient burning or stinging, blurred vision, dry eyes, foreign-body sensation, headache, fatigue, and reduced pulse.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Bronchospasm, respiratory failure, profound bradycardia, heart block, syncope, worsening heart failure, severe hypotension, anaphylaxis, and serious ocular inflammation.
card.interactions: Oral beta blockers, digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, amiodarone, and other rate-slowing drugs increase bradycardia or conduction risk. Catecholamine-depleting drugs increase hypotension. Clonidine withdrawal can be complicated by beta blockade.
card.monitoring: Monitor intraocular pressure, visual symptoms, pulse, blood pressure, respiratory status, heart-failure symptoms, and local eye irritation. Assess for systemic effects in older adults and patients using multiple beta-blocking products.
card.patientTeaching: Use exactly as prescribed even when the eye feels normal. Demonstrate punctal occlusion. Report wheezing, shortness of breath, fainting, swelling, or a very slow pulse. Do not stop suddenly without the eye prescriber.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions. Diabetic patients should monitor glucose carefully because beta blockade may mask adrenergic warning signs of hypoglycemia.

card.indications: Treatment of mild to moderate heart failure and control of resting ventricular rate in adults with chronic atrial fibrillation. Pediatric use includes selected heart-failure patients.
card.mechanism: Inhibits the sodium-potassium ATPase pump, increasing intracellular calcium and myocardial contractility. It also increases vagal tone and slows conduction through the atrioventricular node.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by indication, age, lean body weight, renal function, interacting drugs, and whether a loading dose is required. Typical adult maintenance doses are often 0.125 to 0.25 mg daily, with lower doses in older adults or renal impairment.
card.routeAdministration: Check apical pulse for one full minute before administration and follow hold parameters. Use calibrated devices for oral solution. IV digoxin should be given slowly with cardiac monitoring; avoid IM administration because it is painful and absorption is unreliable.
card.contraindications: Ventricular fibrillation and hypersensitivity to digoxin or other digitalis preparations. Use extreme caution with significant conduction disease, recent myocardial infarction, electrolyte abnormalities, renal impairment, and certain accessory-pathway arrhythmias.
card.warnings: Has a narrow therapeutic index. Toxicity can cause life-threatening bradyarrhythmias or ventricular arrhythmias. Risk rises with renal impairment, advanced age, low potassium, low magnesium, high calcium, low body mass, and interacting drugs.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, anorexia, diarrhea, fatigue, dizziness, headache, and visual changes such as blurred or yellow-green vision.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe bradycardia, heart block, ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation, atrial tachycardia with block, hyperkalemia in acute overdose, delirium, seizures, and fatal toxicity.
card.interactions: Amiodarone, quinidine, verapamil, diltiazem, macrolides, and some P-glycoprotein inhibitors can raise digoxin levels. Loop and thiazide diuretics increase toxicity through hypokalemia. Antacids, cholestyramine, and high-fiber products may reduce absorption.
card.monitoring: Monitor apical pulse and rhythm, ECG when indicated, renal function, potassium, magnesium, calcium, clinical response, and serum digoxin concentration when toxicity, adherence, renal change, or interaction is suspected. Draw levels at the appropriate interval after a dose.
card.patientTeaching: Take at the same time daily and do not double a missed dose. Check pulse if instructed. Report nausea, poor appetite, confusion, new visual changes, palpitations, fainting, or a slow pulse. Keep laboratory appointments.
card.nutrition: Maintain consistent potassium intake as directed and avoid dehydration. Separate high-fiber meals, antacids, or bile-acid sequestrants from the dose when instructed. Do not use potassium supplements or salt substitutes without approval.

card.indications: Treatment of edema associated with heart failure, hepatic cirrhosis, and renal disease, including nephrotic syndrome. Also used alone or with other agents for hypertension.
card.mechanism: Inhibits sodium and chloride reabsorption in the thick ascending limb of the loop of Henle, producing potent diuresis and increased excretion of sodium, chloride, potassium, calcium, magnesium, and water.
card.dosing: Dosing is individualized by indication, volume status, renal function, prior diuretic exposure, and response. Adult oral edema therapy often begins with 20 to 80 mg as a single dose; IV dosing is commonly 20 to 40 mg given slowly and may be repeated or increased. Hypertension often uses divided oral dosing.
card.routeAdministration: Give in the morning; schedule a second dose early in the afternoon to limit nocturia. Measure intake and output, daily weight, blood pressure, and edema. IV administration must be slow to reduce ototoxicity risk, especially at high doses or in renal impairment.
card.contraindications: Anuria and hypersensitivity to furosemide. Use caution with severe electrolyte depletion, hepatic coma or precoma, sulfonamide sensitivity, urinary obstruction, gout, diabetes, and significant renal or hepatic dysfunction.
card.warnings: Potent diuresis can cause profound dehydration, hypovolemia, hypotension, renal injury, and severe electrolyte depletion. Rapid IV administration, high doses, renal impairment, and other ototoxic drugs increase the risk of tinnitus or hearing loss.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Frequent urination, dizziness, orthostatic hypotension, thirst, dehydration, low potassium, low sodium, low magnesium, increased uric acid, and increased glucose.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe volume depletion, acute kidney injury, dangerous electrolyte abnormalities, ventricular dysrhythmias, ototoxicity, pancreatitis, hepatic encephalopathy, agranulocytosis, thrombocytopenia, and severe skin reactions.
card.interactions: Digoxin toxicity increases with hypokalemia. Aminoglycosides and other ototoxic drugs increase hearing risk. NSAIDs can reduce diuretic effect and worsen renal function. Lithium levels can rise. Other antihypertensives increase hypotension.
card.monitoring: Monitor daily weight, intake and output, edema, lung sounds, blood pressure, renal function, sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium, bicarbonate, glucose, uric acid, and hearing symptoms when high-dose IV therapy is used.
card.patientTeaching: Take early in the day, rise slowly, and track daily weight if instructed. Report rapid weight change, severe dizziness, muscle cramps, weakness, palpitations, decreased urine, ringing in the ears, or hearing change.
card.nutrition: Follow prescribed sodium and fluid limits. Maintain potassium-rich foods or supplements only as ordered. Monitor hydration carefully and avoid excessive licorice, which can worsen potassium loss.
